ranking.label: B
ranking.claim: The arrow of time emerges from quantum decoherence, which is inherently directed via the environment-induced superselection (einselection) mechanism.
ranking.mechanism: Open quantum systems interact with their environments, causing loss of phase coherence and effectively irreversible branching into classical pointer states. The direction of increasing entanglement entropy defines the arrow.

ranking.label: C
ranking.claim: The arrow of time is due to the weak interaction's violation of time-reversal symmetry (CP violation).
ranking.mechanism: Although CPT is conserved, CP violation implies T violation. This fundamental asymmetry in particle physics could seed the thermodynamic arrow by creating a tiny bias in early universe processes, which gets amplified by entropy-increasing dynamics.

ranking.label: A
ranking.claim: The arrow of time is a statistical phenomenon: in any large system, entropy will almost certainly increase in both temporal directions from a low-entropy state, but we observe one direction because we are located in a post-initial-condition epoch and our own existence requires low entropy.
ranking.mechanism: Given symmetric statistics, entropy increases both forward and backward from a low-entropy point. However, the universe has only one such point (the Big Bang). Our brains are products of this one low-entropy boundary, so we see the future as the direction away from it. The reversibility objection is avoided because the statistics are not symmetric globally—the boundary condition breaks it.

ranking.label: D
ranking.claim: The arrow is fixed by the (speculative) existence of a fundamental time asymmetry in the laws, such as a preferred direction of time in quantum gravity (e.g., causal sets).
ranking.mechanism: At the Planck scale, spacetime is discrete and causality is built into the structure (e.g., causal set theory). The number of causally related events increases in one direction, defining a global arrow. This asymmetry propagates to macroscopic scales.
